title: Coleataenia scabrida
intro: Coleataenia scabrida, also known as Arizona fescue or scabrida fescue, is a perennial grass species native to the southwestern United States and northern Mexico. The plant has been traditionally used in folk medicine, with reported health benefits including anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties, which may aid in relieving symptoms of colds, fever, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Coleataenia scabrida include phenolic compounds, flavonoids, and alkaloids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. Herbal preparations made from the plant include teas and infusions, as well as topical applications such as salves and ointments, which are used to treat various health conditions, including skin irritations and wounds.
taxonomy: Coleataenia scabrida is a species of grass in the family Poaceae. It is native to the Americas and is found in a variety of habitats, including prairies and open woods. The classification of Coleataenia scabrida is as follows: kingdom Plantae, clade Angiosperms, clade Monocots, clade Commelinids, order Poales, family Poaceae, subfamily Panicoideae, tribe Andropogoneae, and genus Coleataenia. This species is closely related to other grasses in the same genus and family. Further research may be necessary to determine the exact classification and relationships of Coleataenia scabrida.
uses: Coleataenia scabrida, a plant native to the Americas, has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties. The plant's roots and leaves are used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and skin conditions.
